g.data-narration: Welcome to VM Security in Cloud Computing - the foundation of cloud infrastructure protection.
A Virtual Machine is an isolated software emulating a physical computer running its own OS on shared hardware.
g.text: • Isolated software-based emulation of a physical computer
• Runs its own OS and applications on shared physical hardware
• The hypervisor (VMM) creates, manages, and isolates VMs
⚠ A breach can cascade to ALL VMs on the host!

g.data-narration: The virtualization stack shows layered architecture from physical hardware to guest VMs - each layer is an attack surface.
g.text: Guest OS 1 | Guest OS 2 | ... | Guest OS N

g.data-narration: Type 1 hypervisors run directly on hardware (more secure), while Type 2 runs on a host OS (larger attack surface).
g.text: Ex: VMware ESXi, Xen, KVM, Hyper-V
• Runs as app on host OS

g.data-narration: Harden hypervisors by minimizing TCB, securing management interfaces, patching promptly, enabling audit logs, and following CIS benchmarks.
VM isolation is the most critical security property: CPU, memory, I/O, and network isolation prevent cross-VM attacks.
VM Escape is the most severe attack - attacker breaks out of Guest VM to access host and all other VMs by exploiting hypervisor vulnerabilities.
VM sprawl is uncontrolled VM proliferation - forgotten VMs become unpatched security risks with sensitive data and stale credentials.
g.text: VMs created but never decommissioned remain
• VM lifecycle management with automated expiration

g.data-narration: VM images are golden master copies - use trusted sources, pre-harden images, scan for vulnerabilities, never embed secrets, use immutable storage.
g.text: ⚠ NEVER embed secrets in images! Inject at boot using cloud-init, Key Vault, Secrets Manager.

g.data-narration: Live migration moves running VMs between hosts - unencrypted migration transfers sensitive memory contents over the network risking interception.
g.text: VM state (memory with secrets) transferred over network -

g.data-narration: Snapshots capture complete VM state including memory with sensitive data - encrypt snapshot storage, control IAM access, delete old snapshots.
Micro-segmentation creates fine-grained security policies between VMs using SDN - limits lateral movement after breaches, essential for east-west traffic monitoring.

Key takeaways: Hypervisor is most privileged component - Type 1 preferred. VM isolation is critical. VM Escape is the most dangerous attack. Manage lifecycle, images, migration, and snapshots securely.
g.text: 1. Hypervisor = most privileged → Type 1 preferred | 2. VM Isolation is CRITICAL (CPU/Mem/I/O/Net)
3. VM Escape = most severe attack (exploits virtual hw emulation) | 4. Prevent sprawl with lifecycle mgmt
5. Golden images: harden, scan, NEVER embed secrets | 6. Encrypt migration & snapshots

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Know VM Escape mechanics, Type 1 vs Type 2 differences, sprawl risks, golden image concept, isolation mechanisms, and migration/snapshot encryption requirements.
g.text: • VM Escape: Know how it works (exploiting virtual hw emulation) - MOST TESTED TOPIC
• Type 1 vs Type 2: Type 1 more secure due to smaller TCB | • VM Sprawl: unpatched/unmonitored VMs risk
• Isolation: CPU (VT-x), Memory, I/O (VT-d), Network (vSwitch/VLAN) | • Live migration/snapshots = sensitive data exposure
